criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the theoretical framework, including the task definition (Definition 1), lenient-inclusion (Definition 2), and statistical deficiency (Definition 3).
The agent has read Theorem 1 and Theorem 2 to understand the link between 0-deficiency and task inclusion and the ε-deficiency bound on statistical risk across bounded loss functions.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand Information Sufficiency (IS) as a tractable proxy for deficiency, including Equation 1 and its relationship to mutual information.

The agent has read Section 4 on the synthetic experiment to understand the HMM-based data generation, the three task definitions (First, Last, First_or_Last), and the use of attention-layer outputs as representations.

The agent has read Section 4 on the NLP pipeline experiment to understand the five tasks (SYN, SRL, NER, COR, SUM), the OntoNotes dataset split, the four model variants, and the LoRA fine-tuning configuration.
The agent has read Appendix D.1 to extract all hyperparameters for the synthetic experiment, including pre-training and fine-tuning settings shown in Table 6.

The agent has read Appendix D.2 to extract the KNIFE estimator hyperparameters shown in Table 8, including the number of marginal/conditional epochs, learning rates, number of feed-forward layers, number of Gaussian mixture modes, and covariance type.

The agent has read Appendix H to understand the task vector approach, including the LoRA-based task vector definition (Equation 9) and the cosine, L2, and Grassmann distance metrics (Equations 10-11).

The plan includes generating synthetic training data from Hidden Markov Models with a vocabulary of size 10, sequences up to length 30, and producing 11 distinct datasets by varying emission probabilities while keeping the automaton structure fixed.
The plan includes implementing and pre-training a 1-layer transformer language model on HMM-generated sequences in a next-token prediction fashion.
The plan includes fine-tuning the pre-trained transformer on the three classification tasks: First (F), Last (L), and First_or_Last (F∨L).
The plan includes implementing the KNIFE entropy estimator using Gaussian Mixture Models to compute Information Sufficiency between embeddings.
The plan includes computing the 3×3 IS matrix for all pairs of synthetic tasks and averaging results over the 11 HMM datasets.
The plan includes loading the OntoNotes dataset from the broadcast news and newswire subsets with the standard train/validation/test split of 1297/98/97 documents.
The plan includes reformulating all five NLP tasks (SYN, SRL, NER, COR, SUM) as generative tasks as described in Table 1.
The plan includes generating summaries for OntoNotes documents using GPT-3.5 as the annotation source for the SUM task.
The plan includes LoRA fine-tuning of Mistral 7B and Llama 3 8B (both Base and Instruct versions) on each of the five NLP tasks with rank 8 and regularization coefficient α=16.
The plan includes extracting intermediate layer embeddings from layers 10 to 15 of fine-tuned models and computing IS across all task pairs for each of the four model variants.
The plan includes computing Predictive Power (PP) for each task as the sum of directed IS differences relative to all other tasks.
The plan includes a layer ablation study testing different layer ranges (all layers, 10-33, 1-20, 10-15) to validate the layer selection strategy.
The plan includes implementing task vector analysis by computing cosine, L2, and Grassmann distances between LoRA task vectors for each task pair.
The plan includes evaluating cross-task performance using ROUGE and BERTScore and computing Kendall-τ correlation with IS matrices.
The code implements a 6-state Hidden Markov Model with a vocabulary of size 10, generating sequences of up to 30 symbols, with configurable emission probabilities for each state.
The code generates 11 distinct synthetic datasets from 11 different HMMs by varying only the emission probabilities while keeping the underlying automaton (transition structure) fixed.
The code implements the First (F) task: given input (S, C1, C2), return label 0 if C1 equals the first character of S, else label 1.
The code implements the Last (L) task: given input (S, C1, C2), return label 0 if C2 equals the last character of S, else label 1.
The code implements the First_or_Last (F∨L) task: a 4-class classification returning 0 if both C1=first and C2=last, 1 if only C1=first, 2 if only C2=last, and 3 otherwise.
The code implements a 1-layer transformer language model with input size 50, hidden size 100, 1 attention head, and 1 transformer layer for pre-training on HMM-generated sequences.
The code pre-trains the transformer in a next-token prediction fashion with learning rate 2e-3, cosine learning rate scheduler, 100 epochs, and batch size 200.
The code fine-tunes the pre-trained transformer on each of the three classification tasks (F, L, F∨L) with learning rate 2e-4, cosine learning rate scheduler, 100 epochs, and batch size 200.
The code extracts representations exclusively from the output of the attention layer (not the final classification layer) as the task embeddings used for IS computation in the synthetic experiment.

The code implements the KNIFE entropy estimator with the following hyperparameters: 100 marginal training epochs, 100 conditional training epochs, marginal learning rate 1e-4, conditional learning rate 1e-3, 2 feed-forward layers, 8 Gaussian mixture modes for the marginal, 8 Gaussian mixture modes for the conditional, and diagonal covariance.

The code implements Information Sufficiency as defined in Equation 5: IS(Z_U → Z_V) = H_hat(Z_V) - H_hat(Z_V | Z_U), using the KNIFE estimator for entropy and conditional entropy estimation over Gaussian Mixture parametric families.
The code computes the 3×3 IS matrix for all ordered pairs of synthetic tasks (F, L, F∨L) and averages the results across all 11 HMM datasets.
The code verifies pre-training quality by comparing the forward likelihood of the HMM to the empirical likelihood estimated by the transformer, as shown in Figure 5.
The code loads the OntoNotes dataset from the broadcast news and newswire subsets, producing 1297 training documents, 98 validation documents, and 97 test documents.
The code formats the SYN task as a generative task that lists all NP-SBJ (noun phrase subject) syntagms present in the input document.
The code formats the SRL task as a generative task that lists all predicates in the structured form PRED(ARG0, ARG1) from the input document.
The code formats the NER task as a generative task that lists all named entities organized by the eight OntoNotes named entity categories (e.g., event, organization, person, etc.).
The code formats the COR task as a generative task that lists all coreference chains present in the input document.
The code generates document-level summaries using GPT-3.5 for the OntoNotes documents and stores them as the SUM task annotations.
The code loads Mistral 7B (Jiang et al., 2023) in both its Base and Instruct versions for LoRA fine-tuning on the five NLP tasks.
The code loads Llama 3 8B (Dubey et al., 2024) in both its Base and Instruct versions for LoRA fine-tuning on the five NLP tasks.
The code applies LoRA fine-tuning with rank r=8 and regularization coefficient α=16 to each model-task combination, using the HuggingFace peft library.
The code trains each LoRA-adapted model with learning rate 4e-5, a constant learning rate scheduler, for 6 epochs, applying a best-validation-loss model selection strategy at the end of training.
The code extracts hidden state representations from layers 10 to 15 of each fine-tuned LLM to form embeddings Z_U for IS computation.

The code computes the 5×5 IS matrix by averaging IS values over layers 10-15 for all ordered task pairs (SYN, SRL, NER, COR, SUM), repeated for all four model variants (Mistral Base, Mistral Instruct, Llama 3 Base, Llama 3 Instruct), and then averages across models.
The code computes IS between the fine-tuned model and the pre-trained (base) model at each transformer layer in order to identify which layers encode the most task-specific information.
The code implements Predictive Power (PP) as PP(U) = Σ_V [IS(Z_U → Z_V) − IS(Z_V → Z_U)] and ranks tasks by their PP values.
The code evaluates generative task performance using RougeL from the HuggingFace evaluate library for all task-model combinations.
The code applies LoRA specifically to the query (Q) and value (V) projection matrices in each transformer layer of the LLMs.
The code computes task vectors as τ_U = B_U * A_U (the product of the LoRA low-rank matrices), as defined in Equation 9.
The code computes cosine distance between task vectors by flattening and comparing the LoRA weight products flatten(B_U * A_U) and flatten(B_V * A_V).
The code computes L2 distance between task vectors as the Euclidean distance between the flattened LoRA weight products of tasks U and V.
The code computes Grassmann distance between task vectors using the principal angles between the column spaces (image spaces) of the LoRA B matrices, as defined in Equation 10.
The code computes the average task vector distance across all layers separately for the Query and Value projections, as specified in Equation 11.
The code implements cross-task evaluation by applying each fine-tuned model to the other tasks (changing only the output format prompt) and evaluating with both ROUGE and BERTScore metrics.
The code computes Kendall-τ rank correlation between the IS matrix and the cross-task performance matrices (ROUGE and BERTScore) for each of the four model variants.
The code implements the layer ablation study by recomputing IS and PP rankings using at least three alternative layer range configurations: all layers, layers 10-33, and layers 1-20.
The code for the synthetic HMM data generation pipeline and the fine-tuning of the transformer on all three tasks (F, L, F∨L) has been executed successfully without errors.
The KNIFE IS estimator has been executed successfully on the synthetic experiment embeddings to produce IS values for all task pairs.
All 20 model-task fine-tuning runs (4 model variants × 5 NLP tasks) have been executed successfully with LoRA and no runtime errors.
The IS computation across all 5×5 task pairs and layers 10-15 has been executed successfully for all four model variants to produce the IS matrices.
The task vector distance analysis (cosine, L2, Grassmann) for all task pairs and all four model variants has been executed successfully.
The cross-task evaluation using ROUGE and BERTScore has been executed successfully for all model-task combinations.
The layer ablation study has been executed successfully with all specified layer range configurations to produce Tables 10-12.

The IS matrix results for synthetic tasks (Table 2) match the paper: diagonal values are the highest in each row (~0.7-0.8), and IS(F∨L→F) > IS(F→F) and IS(F∨L→L) > IS(L→L), confirming that F∨L includes both F and L.
The IS symmetry property in the synthetic experiment matches the paper: IS(F→F∨L) ≈ IS(L→F∨L) and IS(F∨L→F) ≈ IS(F∨L→L), showing that F and L each carry the same amount of information about F∨L.
The entropy results (Table 5) match the paper: entropy H of F∨L (~0.76) is higher than H of F (~0.66) and H of L (~0.63), explaining why IS values in the F∨L column are elevated.

The RougeL scores (Table 3) match the paper approximately: SYN achieves ~97.3-97.6, SRL ~80-82, NER ~85-88, COR ~54-62, SUM ~49 across Llama 3 Base, Llama 3 Instruct, Mistral Base, and Mistral Instruct.

The layerwise IS comparison (Figure 2) confirms that fine-tuned models diverge most from the pre-trained model in layers 10-15, with the gap IS(pt→ft) − IS(ft→pt) being largest in that range, justifying the layer selection.

The average IS heatmap (Figure 3) matches the paper: the SUM column has the lowest non-diagonal values, the COR column shows low values from all non-COR tasks, and the SYN row exhibits relatively high IS toward other tasks.
The IS ordering property holds as reported in the paper: IS(SYN→SRL) ≤ IS(SRL→SYN) and IS(SRL→NER) ≤ IS(NER→SRL), establishing the lenient inclusion order SYN ⊂ SRL ⊂ NER.

The COR task IS properties match the paper: IS values in the COR column are low (no other task contains information about COR), while IS values in the COR row are high (COR contains information about all other tasks).

The Predictive Power ranking (Table 4) matches the paper: the average PP ranking order across all four models is SUM (4.0) > COR (3.0) > NER (1.5) > SRL (0.75) ≈ SYN (0.75), reflecting the NLP pipeline ordering.

The Kendall-τ correlation results (Table 9) confirm low correlation between IS and naive cross-task evaluation: BERTScore Kendall-τ values are near 0, and ROUGE Kendall-τ values are in the range 0.29-0.43 for all model variants.

The layer ablation results confirm that using all layers (Table 10) or only deep layers (10-33, Table 11) degrades the pipeline ordering by swapping the positions of NER and SRL, while using layers 1-20 (Table 12) restores the correct ordering.

The task vector distance figures (Figures 11-14) show that Grassmann and cosine distances reveal closeness between SYN and SRL task vectors, and the SUM task vector is the most distant from all other tasks.
The per-model IS matrices (Figure 7) are consistent with the averaged heatmap in Figure 3, showing the same structural patterns across all four model variants.
